title: Total Cost of Risk Ownership
summary: The TCRO framework — define, quantify, and defend the complete cost of building risk across an institutional portfolio.
body:
Executive Summary

Institutional real estate is at an inflection point. Insurance markets are harder, emergency repair costs are higher, and boards, lenders, and regulators all want more proof that risk is being managed, not just absorbed. The financial standard most organizations still use to think about risk — Cost of Risk — was built for insurers. It is too narrow for owners.

Total Cost of Risk Ownership (TCRO) is the wider lens. It follows the money through losses, downtime, premiums, operating inefficiency, and governance overhead. Backed by live building data and a common data environment, TCRO turns risk from a report you defend into a number you can move.

This paper defines TCRO, explains why the shift is happening now, and shows what adoption looks like in practice — with three anonymized case studies where owners turned risk visibility into measurable financial outcomes.

Key Findings

  • One avoidable major equipment failure can carry several times its direct repair cost once downtime, emergency work, premium spend, and reputational impact are included. Most of that bill never appears in Cost of Risk.
  • Portfolios with continuous monitoring on critical systems can hold property insurance premium growth at 0% in a hard market by giving underwriters evidence of active control.
  • A digital risk platform layered on existing building systems can compress commissioning timelines from a typical 36-month cycle to roughly 4 months, while avoiding ~2% of infrastructure capital spend.
  • In care environments, predictive air-quality and ventilation control can produce 65% fewer outbreak weeks and a 34% reduction in PPE spending over three years.

The Problem Is Structural, Not Occasional

Most owners experience risk as a series of bad days: a chiller fails, an outbreak hits a community, a leak ruins a tenant space. The instinct is to treat each as an isolated event. The reality is that these events are the visible surface of a structural problem.

That structural problem has three drivers:

  • Building data is fragmented across BMS, CMMS, sensors, spreadsheets, and PDFs — so failure patterns are invisible until they break the surface.
  • Risk is reported in a vocabulary insurers built for themselves (Cost of Risk) — narrow, backward-looking, and indifferent to most of what failures actually cost an owner.
  • Capital planning still runs on asset age, not on risk-weighted economics — so capital is deployed too early in some places and far too late in others.

The cost of this structural problem is rarely a single line item. It is the sum of small inefficiencies, surprise failures, emergency premiums, and avoidable wage costs that erode NOI and weaken the story you can tell at the board and at renewal.

From Cost of Risk to Total Cost of Risk Ownership

Cost of Risk measures what risk costs the insurer — premiums, broker fees, retained claims, and loss-control spend. TCRO measures what risk costs the owner.

For an institutional real estate portfolio, that broader number includes:

  • Losses and claims — direct incident and claim costs.
  • Downtime and business interruption — revenue and service impact when systems are down.
  • Premiums and retentions — insurance spend and retained risk.
  • Operating and maintenance inefficiency — reactive work, wasted labor, emergency premiums.
  • Compliance and reporting burden — the time and cost of proving control.
  • Indirect reputational and governance costs — the trust hit when things go wrong.

Cost of Risk is a slice. TCRO is the bill.

The TCRO Framework

TCRO is built on four practical elements:

1. A common data environment

One source of truth for what you own, what is at risk, and what failure costs. That means a complete, risk-aware asset inventory, normalized definitions across finance, operations, and risk, and traceable lineage from raw signals to board-level KPIs.

2. Continuous risk signal

Monitoring on the systems and conditions where failures hurt most — vibration on critical motors, leak and flow detection on high-risk water lines, air-quality and environmental data in care environments, gas detection on aging infrastructure.

3. Closed-loop workflows

Every signal triggers a clear, prioritized intervention. Every intervention is logged. Every outcome — failure prevented, claim avoided, hours saved — feeds back into the model. Without this loop, you have alerts, not risk management.

4. A financial vocabulary

Every alert, every project, every renewal is expressed in TCRO terms: what risk is being addressed, what it would cost if untreated, what intervention costs, and what the net impact is. This is the language CFOs, boards, brokers, and underwriters share.

The Financial Case for a CFO

TCRO changes three financial conversations:

  • Capital planning moves from age-based replacement to risk-weighted investment. Capital flows to assets where the TCRO reduction per dollar is highest.
  • Insurance stops being a fixed line item. Continuous evidence of control becomes the basis for performance-based program design and pricing.
  • Board reporting shifts from defending failures to allocating against a forward TCRO plan. Directors see a small, stable set of metrics — TCRO level, trend, drivers, avoided losses, capital alignment — that connect today's decisions to tomorrow's outcomes.

For a finance leader, that is the difference between absorbing risk-related costs and engineering them down.

Make Data Pay

Most portfolios already have the raw material — building systems, work orders, sensors, claims, inspections. The asset is stranded because it sits in silos, with no common definitions and no clear financial frame.

When data is governed and connected to TCRO, three categories of return appear:

  • Cost avoidance — claims that never occur, emergency callouts that never get booked, outbreaks that never happen.
  • Cost reduction — lower utility bills through leak detection and better controls, lower insurance premiums over time, less maintenance waste.
  • Value protection and upside — more predictable NOI, stronger ESG and resilience stories for lenders and investors, higher confidence in capital plans.

This is what "make data pay" means in TCRO terms: the same building data that supports operations also lowers risk-related costs and supports stronger capital and insurance outcomes.

Proof

TCRO is not theoretical. Three anonymized client examples illustrate what adoption looks like in practice.

Specialized dementia and long-term care facility. Automated continuous commissioning on the Digital Risk Platform identified uncommissioned drives, misconfigured rooftop units, and under-performing heat exchangers. The result: $93K reduction in handover costs (lower wrap-up liability exposure) and $109,326 in annual operating savings — six figures unlocked from data the building was already producing.
Major Western Canadian events and entertainment campus. Layering monitoring on pumps, motors, gas, water, and environmental conditions across a 50-year-old multi-building campus prevented a maximum probable loss of up to $5M from a single vibration alert on a critical motor. The program also uncovered a previously invisible natural gas leak worth over $400K per year and held property insurance premium growth at 0% in a rising market.
Landmark mixed-use office tower. Automated continuous commissioning reduced startup from the typical 36-month cycle to approximately 4 months, eliminated infrastructure redundancies (saving ~2% of capital), and delivered a 10-year forecast energy savings of $10.1M — over 900 building issues identified and resolved to 10 with clear repair paths.

The common pattern: invisible risk is made visible, signals are tied to financial outcomes, and Total Cost of Risk Ownership is engineered down — not absorbed.

What Adoption Looks Like

Adopting TCRO is a sequence, not a switch.

  • Scope and target. Choose one flagship asset, one campus, or one asset class. Define what TCRO target you are pursuing.
  • Baseline. Build a first-pass TCRO model with your own assumptions for losses, downtime, premiums, operating inefficiency, and governance overhead. Even rough numbers reveal where to focus.
  • Govern. Assign data owners. Standardize definitions for incidents, failures, outages, and interventions. Put a common data environment in place — even a simple one.
  • Instrument. Deploy targeted monitoring on the systems where a single failure could move TCRO meaningfully. Integrate signals with work orders so alerts trigger real action.
  • Close the loop. Track avoided losses and prevented failures in financial terms. Recalculate TCRO periodically. Use the evidence in capital planning and insurance renewals.
  • Scale. Extend to more assets, more asset classes, and more risk domains. Make TCRO part of how the portfolio is run, not a one-off model.

Conclusion

The built environment is moving from "how fast do we recover from failures" to "how precisely do we price and prevent them." TCRO is the financial standard for that shift. It gives owners a number they can defend, a number they can move, and a number that connects directly to the people who live, work, and spend time in their buildings.

Behind every avoided failure is a resident who does not lose heat in winter, a care team that does not work a crisis shift, a visitor whose event is not cancelled. TCRO is how that human impact shows up in the financials.

Failures are predictable. What changes is whether you are positioned to act on them first.

Novem predicts and prevents equipment failures before they happen so leaders can reduce Total Cost of Risk Ownership, protect cash flow, and manage risk-related costs with confidence. title: Automated Continuous Commissioning Unlocks $200K+ At A Specialized Dementia Care Facility
client: A specialized dementia and long-term care facility
summary: From manual commissioning to automated continuous commissioning that cuts wrap-up liability and delivers six-figure annual savings.
body:
Automated Continuous Commissioning Unlocks $200K+ At A Specialized Dementia Care Facility

From manual commissioning to automated continuous commissioning that cuts wrap-up liability and delivers six-figure annual savings.

Solution Provider: Novem Digital | Customer: A specialized dementia and long-term care facility in Western Canada | Location: Western Canada | Asset: 4-storey, purpose-built dementia and long-term care facility with underground parking

BACKGROUND: High-Acuity Care Requires Reliable, Efficient Buildings

A specialized dementia and long-term care facility provides care for more than 200 residents in a 4-storey building above a large underground parkade. The completed construction cost was approximately $53M, with complex mechanical and electrical systems needed to support vulnerable residents.

Leadership needed to ensure the building performed as designed, both to protect residents and to prove financial performance to owners and insurers.

THE CHALLENGE: Traditional Commissioning Leaves Value On The Table

Standard commissioning practices often leave issues undetected until after handover, when they become more expensive to fix and harder to quantify. For this facility, any underperformance would affect comfort, energy use, and ultimately Total Cost of Risk Ownership.

Key risks included:

  • Mechanical equipment not running as specified
  • Air handling issues affecting pressurization and comfort
  • Undetected control problems driving up energy use and wear

The owner needed a way to validate performance in real time, document issues for insurance and construction teams, and avoid paying for hidden inefficiencies.

THE SOLUTION: Automated Continuous Commissioning On The Digital Risk Platform

Novem Digital deployed its Automated Continuous Commissioning (ACC) solution, enabled by the Digital Risk Platform, across key building systems.

Analytics On Air Handling And Pressurization

Analytics monitored fan drives in air handling units and flagged units that were not operational. Investigation revealed drives that had not been properly commissioned and could not run. Correcting these issues ensured proper pressurization and prevented cold air from infiltrating resident spaces.

ACC also identified rooftop units that were not responding correctly to heating calls. With digital forensics, contractors resolved the control issues, allowing equipment to function as intended.

Energy Waste And Filter Management

Analytics flagged air filter pressure drops indicating dirty filters. In response, technicians replaced filters before fans ran at higher speeds to compensate, reducing wasted energy and stress on equipment.

Heat Exchanger And Critical Equipment Monitoring

Heat exchanger analytics revealed supply temperatures below expected levels. Further investigation found manual isolation valves closed and circulation pumps overridden off at the local panel. Once corrected, heating performance returned to specifications.

Laundry dryer monitoring identified false run status signals and issues with lint traps and door switches. Continuous monitoring ensured this critical support equipment remained functional and compliant with vendor specifications.

BY THE NUMBERS: The Impact

  • $93K reduction in costs during the handover process, demonstrating a reduction in wrap-up liability premiums
  • $109,326 in annual operating savings enabled by Novem’s Digital Risk Platform and ACC

WHAT THE NUMBERS MEAN

Implementing Automated Continuous Commissioning proved that commissioning can be both a risk control and a financial lever. The $93K handover saving reduced wrap-up liability costs and provided evidence that performance issues were detected and resolved before they turned into claims or chronic operating inefficiencies.

Annual operating savings above $100K strengthen cash flow and support reinvestment in resident programs, staffing, and reserves. Leadership can now quantify how improved building performance supports both care quality and financial resilience.

In practice, the facility is monetizing data. Commissioning and performance data now translate directly into lower risk-related costs, reduced insurance exposure, and higher operating efficiency, turning what used to be a cost of doing business into a recurring financial return.

THE TRANSFORMATION: From Hidden Issues To Measured Performance

For owners and insurers: The facility now has data that validates performance for insurance, owners, and construction teams, demonstrating that risk is actively managed rather than assumed.

For operators and facilities teams: Staff rely on continuous analytics to catch issues early, schedule corrective work efficiently, and keep critical systems operating within specification.

For residents and families: While they may never see the analytics directly, residents benefit from more stable temperatures, more reliable systems, and fewer disruptions, all in a facility that runs closer to its intended performance.

LOOKING FORWARD: Scaling ACC As Standard Practice

The success at this facility positions Automated Continuous Commissioning as a standard for future projects and retrofits in the operator’s portfolio. By treating commissioning as a continuous process rather than a one-time event, organizations can protect cash flow, reduce Total Cost of Risk Ownership, and provide better environments for high-acuity residents.

title: 65% Fewer Outbreak Weeks At A Regional Seniors Living Operator
summary: From 19 weeks of lockdowns to 3, restoring safe family connection and reducing outbreak-related costs.
body:
65% Fewer Outbreak Weeks At A Regional Seniors Living Operator

From 19 weeks of lockdowns to 3, restoring safe family connection and reducing outbreak-related costs.

Solution Provider: Novem Digital | Customer: A regional seniors living operator with a 120-resident supportive living community | Location: Western Canada | Asset: Supportive and long-term care residence

BACKGROUND: Keeping Residents Safe, Connected, And Out Of Isolation

A regional seniors living operator manages communities where residents depend on stable routines, familiar connections, and safe social contact. During one flu season, a 120-resident site endured 19 consecutive weeks of outbreak restrictions.

Residents went weeks without in-person family visits. Meals moved back into rooms, group activities stopped, and staff worked through constant anxiety about the next outbreak. The operator needed a way to protect residents without repeating the pattern of prolonged isolation.

THE CHALLENGE: Protect Without Isolating

Before the transformation, respiratory outbreaks spread rapidly and triggered lockdown protocols that were blunt by necessity. Poor ventilation and outdated infection-control practices meant leaders could only see risk after people were already sick.

The result:

  • Weeks of restricted movement, loneliness, and lost autonomy for residents
  • Mental exhaustion for employees managing repeated crises
  • Families forced to watch loved ones through windows instead of visiting in person

Leadership recognized they were managing symptoms instead of causes. They could not see what was happening in the air until it was too late. They needed real-time visibility and prediction, not faster reaction to each crisis.

THE SOLUTION: Predicting Viral Risk Before It Becomes Crisis

The operator partnered with Novem Digital to deploy a digital risk platform focused on air quality and infection risk. The mission was clear: restore human connection while keeping residents, staff, and families safe.

Continuous Air Monitoring Everyone Can See

Novem deployed advanced sensors throughout the building to track CO2, humidity, temperature, particulate matter, volatile organic compounds, and other environmental factors tied to respiratory virus survival.

The platform monitors air quality in real time and surfaces risk conditions to staff instead of burying data in back-office systems. The operator is adding resident-facing displays that translate complex data into simple messages such as “Air Quality: Safe” or “Ventilation Increasing To Reduce Risk,” making protection visible to residents and families.

Predictive Intelligence That Prevents Outbreaks

Novem’s platform uses machine learning to identify combinations of environmental conditions that historically precede viral spread at the site. When those patterns emerge, the system flags elevated risk before people become sick.

Automated actions include:

  • Increasing ventilation in affected areas
  • Alerting staff with clear guidance for preventive measures
  • Updating onscreen guidance for residents and visitors
  • Logging interventions for performance tracking and insurance evidence

For residents, that means fewer disruptions and more family time. For employees, it means dealing with issues during regular shifts instead of managing weekend crisis events. For families, it offers confidence that safety does not require isolation.

A Platform Designed To Evolve

The operator chose Novem Digital’s open protocol platform to integrate future capabilities such as weather data, broader air quality inputs, and potential wearable integrations. The goal is a long-term foundation for safety, wellness, and capital planning, not a single-purpose point solution.

BY THE NUMBERS: The Impact

Headline results at the community:

  • 65% fewer outbreak weeks
  • 65% lower outbreak-related wage premiums
  • 34% reduction in PPE spending over 3 years
  • Zero respiratory outbreaks for 12 consecutive months after installation
  • Approximately 200 residents and 110 employees directly benefited, along with hundreds of family members and visitors

WHAT THE NUMBERS MEAN

These savings allowed the operator to redirect funding from crisis response toward resident programming, wellness services, and employee support. Instead of absorbing outbreak-related wage premiums and PPE costs every season, leadership can plan capital and operating budgets with clearer visibility into risk drivers.

For insurers and boards, the data demonstrates that outbreak risk is measurable and manageable, not simply a seasonal inevitability. That evidence supports better insurance positioning and more confident decisions about coverage structure and capital allocation.

At the same time, residents and employees describe the system as passive and non-invasive. Many simply notice fewer worries about illness and more confidence heading into respiratory season.

The operator is also starting to monetize data. Air quality, outbreak, and staffing data now inform staffing models, program design, and insurance negotiations, turning what used to be unstructured information into a financial asset that reduces Total Cost of Risk Ownership.

THE BREAKTHROUGH MOMENT: A Season Without Respiratory Outbreaks

In a recent season, the community experienced zero respiratory outbreak weeks for the first time in more than seven years. Cherished traditions like seasonal teas and school performances proceeded exactly as planned.

For residents who had lived through repeated cancellations, that season represented more than a metric. It was proof that daily life could feel normal again while still being protected.

THE TRANSFORMATION: Life Improves For Everyone

For residents: Outbreak season no longer automatically means weeks of room-based isolation. Residents experience safer visits with family, restored group activities, and a renewed sense of freedom and dignity.

For families: Visits are more predictable, even during respiratory season. Families know their loved ones are protected by an environment that adjusts itself before risks become crises.

For frontline employees: Early warning systems relieve some of the mental load that came with constant crisis mode. Staff can focus more on care and connection instead of emergency protocols and overtime coverage.

For the broader community: The facility demonstrates that senior care can be both safe and connected, rebuilding trust after years of pandemic disruption.

LOOKING FORWARD: A Model For Post-Pandemic Senior Living

The operator is now exploring expansion of the model across its portfolio and sharing lessons with peer organizations. With Novem Digital’s platform as a foundation, leadership can integrate additional data sources and demonstrate how proactive risk management supports dignity, connection, and financial resilience.

title: Preventing A $5M Claim At A Major Western Canadian Events Campus
client: A major Western Canadian events and entertainment campus
summary: From reactive repairs on 50-year-old infrastructure to predictive operations that hold premiums flat and unlock over $400K per year in utility savings.
body:
Preventing A $5M Claim At A Major Western Canadian Events Campus

From reactive repairs on 50-year-old infrastructure to predictive operations that hold premiums flat and unlock over $400K per year in utility savings.

Solution Provider: Novem Digital | Customer: A major Western Canadian events and entertainment campus with multi-building, aging infrastructure | Location: Western Canada | Asset: Multi-building events and entertainment campus

BACKGROUND: Protecting An Iconic Campus With Aging Infrastructure

A major events and entertainment campus hosts millions of visitors each year across multiple buildings, outdoor spaces, and attractions. Many core buildings are more than 50 years old, with the newest completed in 1970.

The campus relied on manual inspections and reactive repairs. Traditional building automation systems followed a reactive operating model with limited ability to predict risk of failure in critical systems. Leadership faced a complex mix of aging equipment, deferred maintenance, and rising insurance and utility costs.

THE CHALLENGE: Can Old Infrastructure Become Predictive Without A Full Replacement

Leadership needed to answer three urgent questions:

  • Can we reduce property and personal injury claims
  • Can we reduce unscheduled downtime of critical infrastructure
  • Can we control utility costs and leak losses

Wholesale infrastructure replacement was not financially realistic. But continuing to operate blind meant absorbing preventable losses, emergency repair premiums, and volatile property insurance costs. Leadership needed proof that existing systems still had useful life if they could make invisible risk visible.

THE SOLUTION: Layering A Digital Risk Platform On Existing Systems

The organization partnered with Novem Digital to deploy a digital risk platform across its highest-value, highest-risk assets without ripping out existing systems.

Instrumenting Critical Systems

Novem and the campus team focused on:

  • Vibration and condition monitoring on pumps and motors in the ice-making plant
  • Water leak and flow detection at high-risk locations
  • Natural gas leak detection via a seismic shut-off valve and associated monitoring
  • Real-time decibel monitoring across outdoor event spaces
  • Temperature, door, and motor status sensors on walk-in coolers

In parallel, the campus modernized lighting, replacing thousands of aging fixtures with high-efficiency programmable lights, reducing fire risk and cutting electrical use by approximately 30%.

Continuous Monitoring Of What Matters Most

The Novem platform continuously monitored the condition of critical mechanical systems, water flow, and sound levels across the campus. Maintenance staff received early warnings when equipment entered distress, allowing planned interventions during normal hours instead of emergency repair calls at premium rates.

Event managers gained access to real-time noise profiles so they could adjust sound levels during events instead of waiting for complaints. Integrated lightning detection improved public and staff safety during outdoor events.

Predictive Intelligence That Prevents Losses

The platform did more than monitor. It predicted failures. Vibration monitoring on a key motor in the ice-making plant identified early signs of failure and triggered a preventive maintenance intervention. That single event prevented a maximum probable loss estimated up to $5M in property damage, business interruption, and future premium impact.

A natural gas leak worth more than $400K per year was discovered and resolved after the installation of a seismic shut-off valve as part of the broader digital risk program. Underground water leaks were detected and mitigated, saving over 10,000 liters since baseline.

BY THE NUMBERS: The Impact

Key outcomes across the campus:

  • Up to $5M maximum probable insurance claim prevented through predictive maintenance on ice-making equipment
  • 0% annual increase in property insurance premiums despite broader market inflation
  • Over $400K per year in natural gas losses identified and recovered
  • More than 10,000 liters of water leaks detected and resolved
  • Approximately 30% reduction in electrical use from lighting modernization, with a 4.5 year payback on the lighting project, excluding insurance benefits

WHAT THE NUMBERS MEAN

These results allowed the organization to shift funding from emergency repairs and unplanned losses into capital planning, visitor experience improvements, and staff support. Insurance partners saw measurable risk reduction, which strengthened underwriting confidence and created a foundation for future risk-adjusted pricing.

By treating insurance as a controllable cost, leadership demonstrated that aging infrastructure can achieve predictable Total Cost of Risk Ownership. Predictive operations also improved cash flow visibility, making long-term capital decisions easier to defend.

The campus is explicitly monetizing data. Sensor data on vibration, gas, water, sound, and energy now supports claim prevention, premium control, and ESG reporting, turning operational data into a financial asset that pays for itself through avoided losses and improved terms.

THE BREAKTHROUGH MOMENT: Discovering The Invisible $400K Leak

The installation of a seismic shut-off valve revealed a major natural gas leak that had gone undetected for years, valued at more than $400K per year.

In previous years, that leak would have continued indefinitely, hidden inside aggregate utility bills. Once monitoring exposed the anomaly, the organization could quantify, fix, and verify the improvement. That single discovery effectively paid for the system and validated the entire digital risk strategy.

THE TRANSFORMATION: Life Improves For Visitors, Staff, And The Community

For visitors: Upgraded lighting and real-time monitoring directly support public safety. Visitors benefit from better visibility, reduced fire risk, and assurance that the campus monitors weather and environmental conditions during outdoor events.

For staff and operations: Maintenance teams moved from constant firefighting to a more predictable workload. They can act on early warnings, schedule repairs during planned downtime, and rely on data rather than assumptions.

For the community and regulators: Real-time decibel monitoring transformed relationships with the local municipality by providing transparent data that showed many complaints were tied to external traffic rather than events. Insurance partners now see a campus that actively manages risk, not just carries coverage.

LOOKING FORWARD: Fully Digitizing The Campus

The campus plans to expand Novem’s digital risk platform across all buildings to further improve capital planning, insurance positioning, and long-term operating costs. As more systems are instrumented, leadership will have a single source of truth for risk and performance across the portfolio.

title: From 36 To 4 Months: Accelerated Commissioning At A Landmark Mixed-Use Office Tower
summary: From multi-year manual commissioning to an integrated digital risk platform that reduces capital spend and delivers a 10-year energy savings forecast of $10.1M.
body:
From 36 To 4 Months: Accelerated Commissioning At A Landmark Mixed-Use Office Tower

From multi-year manual commissioning to an integrated digital risk platform that reduces capital spend and delivers a 10-year energy savings forecast of $10.1M.

Solution Provider: Novem Digital | Customer: A landmark mixed-use office tower in a major Canadian city | Location: Canada | Asset: Mixed-use office tower with integrated plant and systems

BACKGROUND: A Flagship Smart Building With Complex Systems

A landmark mixed-use office tower in a major Canadian city was designed as a sustainability and technology showpiece. The site integrates a shared central plant, photovoltaic array, uninterruptible power supplies, generators, HVAC, security, lighting, car chargers, life safety, operable windows, and irrigation systems.

These integrated systems generate large volumes of data that must be managed in real time if the building is to meet its performance targets and brand promise.

THE CHALLENGE: Manual Commissioning On A Highly Integrated Asset

The developer’s previous projects relied on a manual commissioning process that typically took 2 years and cost approximately $300K. For this tower, the complexity of integrated systems created operational risk and the potential for extended commissioning timelines.

Key challenges included:

  • Integrating multiple vendor systems into an open, secure platform
  • Aligning diverse stakeholders across finance, design, technical, construction, operations, and HR
  • Establishing an information management program including alarm management and performance scorecards
  • Educating stakeholders on the value of a proactive, predictive operating model

Leadership wanted to accelerate commissioning, improve performance, reduce wrap-up liability costs, and prove the long-term financial value of the investment.

THE SOLUTION: Digital Risk Platform And Automated Continuous Commissioning

Novem Digital deployed its Digital Risk Platform with Automated Continuous Commissioning to reduce commissioning timelines and turn integrated systems into a source of financial insight.

Identifying And Resolving Issues At Scale

After the platform was implemented, analytics identified more than 900 issues across building systems. Over roughly 4 months, Novem’s algorithms, combined with targeted reporting, helped the project team reduce the issue count from more than 900 to 10, each with a clear path to repair.

By capturing and resolving issues early, the project team improved building performance while reducing risk of future claims and operating inefficiencies.

Accelerated Commissioning And Reduced Capital Spend

Automated continuous commissioning reduced the commissioning and optimization period from the typical 36-month cycle to approximately 4 months. The approach also allowed the team to eliminate infrastructure redundancies and save approximately 2% of infrastructure capital.

The project delivered energy intensification roughly one third below peer office towers in the same market, supporting a 10-year forecast energy savings of approximately $10.1M.

BY THE NUMBERS: The Impact

  • Commissioning reduced from 36 months to approximately 4 months
  • More than 900 building issues identified and reduced to 10 with clear resolution paths
  • Approximately 2% of infrastructure capital spend avoided through design and integration efficiencies
  • Energy intensification approximately one third lower than peer towers, supporting a 10-year savings forecast of $10.1M

WHAT THE NUMBERS MEAN

By compressing commissioning timelines, the developer reduced wrap-up liability exposure and began operating at target performance levels much earlier. Avoided capital spend and energy savings directly improve project returns and support a stronger long-term Total Cost of Risk Ownership profile.

For executives, the building now functions as a proof point that integrated smart systems can deliver measurable financial outcomes, not just a sustainability story. Performance data underpins a 10-year, $10.1M energy savings forecast and validates infrastructure savings at the board and lender level.

This is a clear example of monetizing data. Real-time and historical performance data are used to cut commissioning time, avoid capital spend, and prove energy performance, turning the building’s data exhaust into a financial asset over the life of the tower.

THE TRANSFORMATION: From Data Overload To Predictive Operations

For owners and investors: The tower demonstrates that a digital risk platform can transform a complex smart building into a predictable financial asset, with transparent performance data for boards, lenders, and tenants.

For operators and facility teams: Embedded analytics and automated continuous commissioning help staff detect inefficiencies, identify potential risk areas, and act proactively to keep systems within specification.

For tenants: While most will never see the technical details, they benefit from improved comfort, reduced downtime, and the assurance that the building is operated to high environmental and operational standards.

LOOKING FORWARD: A Template For Future Developments

The developer can now apply this approach to future projects, using the Digital Risk Platform as a repeatable template to accelerate commissioning, control capital spend, and demonstrate energy performance. Over time, the portfolio benefits from consistent data and a shared operating model.

title: 10% Insurance Savings and 30% Digital Infrastructure Savings for an Independent Seniors Housing Developer
client: A Western Canadian independent seniors housing developer
summary: From escalating premiums and overruns to lower Total Cost of Risk Ownership and stronger Net Operating Income on a new community.
body:
10% Insurance Savings and 30% Digital Infrastructure Savings for an Independent Seniors Housing Developer

From escalating premiums and overruns to lower Total Cost of Risk Ownership and stronger Net Operating Income on a new community.

Solution Provider: Novem Digital | Customer: A Western Canadian independent seniors housing developer | Location: Western Canada | Asset: New mid-market independent living community

BACKGROUND: Protecting Margins In A Tight Seniors Housing Market

A Western Canadian independent seniors housing developer designs, builds, operates, and retains mid-market communities in a market where construction inflation and compressed margins put every project under scrutiny. Leadership knew that even a 1% change in total project cost could materially shift returns and exit value.

At the same time, resident expectations around safety, air quality, and reliability have increased while staffing remains tight. The developer needed a way to reduce risk-related costs and protect cash flow without compromising resident experience.

THE CHALLENGE: Rising Risk Costs Treated As A Fixed Line Item

Before engaging Novem Digital, the developer faced three converging pressures.

  • Insurance premiums across course of construction, wrap-up liability, and post-occupancy programs were volatile and hard to forecast, even on projects delivered on time and without claims.
  • Construction budgets were under pressure from global cost drivers the team could not control, eroding returns before buildings opened.
  • Resident expectations around air quality and battery-driven appliances were increasing fire and safety risk at the same time as margins were tightening.

Internally, risk-related costs were treated as something to absorb rather than something that could be engineered and reduced with data. The owner needed clear answers to basic questions: How is risk being controlled, who owns the data, and how does it translate into lower Total Cost of Risk Ownership and better insurance positioning.

THE SOLUTION: Three Policies, One Lifecycle, Enabled By A Digital Risk Platform

The developer partnered with Novem Digital and a strategic insurance broker to manage construction and operational risk as a single, data-driven lifecycle instead of a series of disconnected decisions.

Coordinated Insurance Lifecycle

Novem and the broker helped design and sequence course of construction, wrap-up liability, and post-occupancy property programs as one continuous financial tool for the asset. All three placements were aligned, informed by shared risk data, and structured to recognize proven improvement over time instead of assuming worst-case scenarios.

Digital Risk Platform And Converged Infrastructure

Novem deployed a Digital Risk Platform that spans both construction and operations, anchored by a converged optical infrastructure and targeted monitoring of key risks.

The platform focused on:

  • Water systems and water damage risk
  • Security monitoring
  • Mechanical and electrical construction quality
  • Arc flash and fire risk
  • Air quality in resident spaces

Instead of siloed systems, the owner, operations, finance, broker, and insurers gained a shared, structured view of risk drivers and operating performance.

Predictive Operations And Productivity

Success metrics were defined in financial and operational terms: reduced Total Cost of Risk Ownership, protection of cash flow, and support for stronger staff to resident ratios. Early visibility and prioritized actions allowed teams to shift from reactive problem solving to predictive operations, so staff could focus on resident care while avoiding surprise costs.

BY THE NUMBERS: The Impact

  • 10% reduction in insurance costs across construction and post-occupancy programs
  • 30% reduction in digital infrastructure capital spend versus the original budget
  • 1% reduction in total construction costs when insurance and digital infrastructure savings are combined

These improvements were achieved without compromising safety, coverage, or resident experience.

WHAT THE NUMBERS MEAN

The 10% insurance reduction proves that insurance is no longer a fixed, uncontrollable expense. By sequencing three programs as one lifecycle and backing them with continuous risk data, the owner can work with brokers and carriers using measured performance rather than assumptions.

The 30% digital infrastructure saving demonstrates that a smarter approach to platform, connectivity, and monitoring frees capital without increasing risk. That capital can be redirected into wellness features, contingency reserves, or additional care capacity.

Combined, these effects reduce total construction cost by 1%. At portfolio scale, that supports stronger project returns, more predictable Total Cost of Risk Ownership, and higher asset values while protecting the standard of care that residents and families expect.

Critically, the developer is now monetizing data. Construction and operational risk data has become a negotiating asset with insurers and lenders, converting better real-world performance into lower premiums, better terms, and more defensible capital decisions.

THE TRANSFORMATION: Life Improves For Owners, Partners, And Residents

For owners and investors: The developer now has a repeatable approach to design risk-related costs into every project rather than absorbing them after the fact. With a single risk dataset and audit-ready evidence from Novem’s platform, leaders can answer board and lender questions about resilience, wellness, and construction risk with one clear narrative.

For finance, operations, brokers, and insurers: All stakeholders work from the same evidence instead of disconnected reports. The broker can present underwriting-grade data that supports better terms, including the 10% premium reduction already achieved. Continuous performance data informs decisions on deductibles, limits, and structures as more history accumulates.

For residents, families, and staff: Behind the financial outcomes is a more stable environment for seniors living independently. Fewer preventable failures and disruptions mean fewer unit closures, fewer emergency repairs that displace residents, and less time spent in crisis mode by staff. Families gain confidence that buildings are monitored and managed proactively, not left to chance.

LOOKING FORWARD: A Model For Independent Seniors Living

The developer is now applying this model across its independent living portfolio in Western Canada. Each new project is designed from day one with a Digital Risk Platform, coordinated insurance lifecycle, and continuous monitoring that makes failures predictable.

As performance evidence grows, the owner expects to further strengthen insurance positioning, improve capital planning accuracy, and support strategic discussions with lenders and partners.

CONFIDENTIALITY AND NDA

This case study is anonymized to protect customer confidentiality. Under NDA, Novem Digital can share full customer identity, underwriting evidence, and detailed financial and technical results with qualified insurers, lenders, and strategic partners.

ABOUT NOVEM DIGITAL

Novem Digital is an AI powered digital risk platform that transforms operational data from real estate into measurable financial returns. Novem predicts and prevents equipment failures before they happen so leaders can reduce Total Cost of Risk Ownership, protect cash flow, and manage risk-related costs with confidence.

title: Total Cost of Risk Ownership vs Cost of Risk
summary: Most institutional owners still manage risk using a number built for insurers, not for them. That number is Cost of Risk – premiums, claims, deductibles...
body:
Most institutional owners still manage risk using a number built for insurers, not for them. That number is Cost of Risk – premiums, claims, deductibles, retentions. It is familiar. It is also too narrow for a CFO responsible for an entire portfolio.

Total Cost of Risk Ownership, or TCRO, is the wider lens. Where Cost of Risk stops at the edge of the insurance policy, TCRO follows the money all the way through downtime, emergency work, governance, and capital timing. In a world where boards, lenders, and regulators demand more proof, that wider lens is no longer optional.

For people who know the traditional term: TCRO extends the idea of Total Cost of Risk (TCOR) into a broader owner-level standard.

What does Cost of Risk miss in real estate?

Cost of Risk only counts what the insurer sees: premiums, broker fees, retained claims, and loss-control costs. It ignores the majority of what risk really costs a real estate portfolio.

The missing pieces show up in places like:

  • Lost revenue and NOI when critical systems fail during peak periods
  • Emergency callout premiums and rush-order parts
  • Business interruption that never becomes a formal insurance claim
  • Capital projects forced forward by failures instead of planned on condition
  • Manual effort spent reconciling reports for boards, lenders, and regulators

For a single major equipment failure, every $1 of direct repair cost can carry several dollars of total impact once downtime, emergency work, and knock-on effects are included. Only a fraction of that appears in the traditional Cost of Risk line.

If you report only what the insurer can see, you are managing the smallest part of the bill.

What is Total Cost of Risk Ownership?

Total Cost of Risk Ownership reframes risk as a full financial number for owners and CFOs. It measures what risk costs the portfolio, not just what it costs to transfer part of it to an insurer.

A practical TCRO view in real estate usually includes:

  • Losses and claims – direct incident and claim costs
  • Downtime and business interruption – revenue and service impact when systems are down
  • Premiums and retentions – insurance spend and retained risk
  • Operating and maintenance inefficiency – reactive work, wasted labor, emergency premiums
  • Compliance and reporting burden – the time and cost of proving control
  • Indirect reputational and governance costs – the trust hit when things go wrong

Name those costs. Quantify them. Trend them. Now risk is a line you can move, not just a report you defend.

If you want the full breakdown of each component, the TCRO white paper goes deeper on the framework.

Why this shift is no longer optional

For years, it was possible to treat Cost of Risk as “good enough”. That is changing.

Three forces are driving the shift:

  • Harder insurance markets that are more sensitive to risk quality, especially in property.
  • Higher emergency and replacement costs that increase the penalty for unplanned failures.
  • Higher scrutiny from boards and regulators, who expect auditable evidence of control, not just narrative assurance.

If you are still steering with a metric that ignores most of the economic impact, you are choosing to fly with partial instruments.

For a deeper look at how governance supports this shift, see Data Governance Is the Operating System of Financial Control.

How does TCRO change decisions for CFOs?

TCRO turns risk from a narrow insurance metric into a comprehensive financial lever. Once you see downtime, emergency work, and governance costs in the same frame as premiums and claims, decision-making shifts.

In practice, portfolios that adopt TCRO with a live data foundation see three changes:

  • Capital planning moves from age-based to risk-weighted. Live asset data and predictive operations let you replace equipment when condition and TCRO justify it, not when a spreadsheet says it is due.
  • Insurance becomes a performance negotiation. When you can show continuous monitoring, documented interventions, and reduced loss experience, property insurance behaves less like a fixed tax and more like a controllable cost inside TCRO.
  • Board conversations shift from defence to allocation. Instead of explaining failures, you can show avoided losses, what they would have cost, and where you are investing next.

If you want to see how this plays out in practice, From Reactive to Predictive: Why Your Maintenance Model Is Now a Finance Strategy walks through specific operational shifts.

What data do you need to measure TCRO credibly?

TCRO is only as strong as its inputs. To treat it as a living number rather than a one-off model, you need three kinds of data in one place:

  • Live operational data from critical systems and environmental conditions
  • Normalized loss and performance history – incidents, claims, near-misses
  • A complete asset inventory – age, condition, replacement cost, and business importance

Taken together, this is the common data environment that supports both TCRO and predictive operations. In that environment, a vibration anomaly or air-quality deviation is not just an alarm; it is a data point tied to probability, expected loss, and a time window based on real experience.

We unpack that data foundation in more detail in Data Governance Is Not an IT Project. It Is the Operating System of Financial Control.

What should you ask inside your organization?

At your next risk or capital-planning meeting, three questions can reveal how mature your TCRO picture really is:

  • What is our best estimate of Total Cost of Risk Ownership today – not just Cost of Risk?
  • How much of that number is driven by unplanned failures, emergency work, and avoidable downtime?
  • What continuous data do we trust today to change that curve?

The answers will show whether your organization is managing the right number, or just the convenient one.

Where the industry needs to move

The built environment is moving from “how fast do we recover from failures” to “how precisely do we price and prevent them.” Portfolios that adopt TCRO early, and back it with real data, will be the ones that:

  • Reduce surprise costs
  • Protect EBITDA and asset value
  • Earn greater confidence from boards, lenders, and insurers

Behind every avoided failure is a resident who does not lose heat in winter, a care team that does not work a crisis shift, a building that keeps its trust. TCRO is how that human impact shows up in the numbers.

If you want to see how TCRO changes capital planning specifically, read How TCRO Changes Capital Planning: From Age-Based Replacement to Risk-Weighted Investment.

Where to go from here

If you want to see how TCRO could look in your own portfolio, you have two practical ways to start:

  • Model the portfolio yourself. Use the TCRO calculator on the TCRO page to plug in your own assumptions for failures, downtime, and premiums and see how they move Total Cost of Risk Ownership.
  • Get a directional, underwriter-informed estimate. If you have a flagship asset in mind, use Estimate Your Savings. Share a single property and within one business day a specialist – together with an insurance partner – will return a reviewed estimate of what predictive operations and risk data could change in your TCRO picture.

From there, TCRO stops being an abstract idea and becomes a number your team can move on purpose.

title: Data Governance Is Not an IT Project. It Is the Operating System of Financial Control.
summary: Most portfolios do not suffer from a lack of data. They suffer from a lack of ownership. No one is quite sure who owns which...
body:
Most portfolios do not suffer from a lack of data. They suffer from a lack of ownership. No one is quite sure who owns which metrics, where the data lives, or whether it can be trusted. The consequences rarely show up as a headline failure. They show up as friction.

Dashboards are questioned. Capital decisions drag on. Teams maintain three versions of the same number. Insurance submissions go in light on evidence. Board reports are assembled by hand and argued the night before they are due. That is not a technology issue. It is a financial control issue.

Data governance, done properly, is the operating system that controls all of this.

What is data governance in institutional real estate?

In institutional real estate, data governance is the discipline that makes portfolio data reliable, traceable, and usable for financial decisions.

In practical terms, it answers four questions:

  • Who is accountable for which data and KPIs?
  • What do key terms mean, and how are they used across finance, operations, and risk?
  • How do we ensure the numbers are accurate, complete, and traceable?
  • Who has access to which data, and under what conditions?

When those questions are settled, the organization gains something simple and rare: one version of the truth that can be defended when a board, lender, or insurer asks “How do you know?”

For the financial framing that sits on top of this, see Total Cost of Risk Ownership vs Cost of Risk.

What happens when data governance fails?

When governance is weak, costs rise quietly.

Patterns are consistent across institutional portfolios:

  • Maintenance spend drifts up because asset data is incomplete or unreliable.
  • Decisions slow down because numbers cannot be trusted without manual checks.
  • Insurance is priced on uncertainty instead of demonstrated control.
  • Capital projects are harder to defend, weakening board and lender confidence.
  • Emergency repair premiums compound because failure is not predicted early.

When a chiller fails without warning, the cost is not just the replacement invoice. It is emergency labor, tenant impact, potential claims, and governance exposure when someone asks “Did we see this coming?” For a single major event, total impact can easily reach many times the direct repair cost.

Those costs belong inside Total Cost of Risk Ownership (TCRO), not in a miscellaneous bucket. Without governance, you cannot measure them properly.

How does data governance support TCRO and AI?

TCRO and AI both depend on trustworthy inputs. Without governance:

  • TCRO becomes an estimate assembled from conflicting numbers.
  • AI models amplify bad data, producing outputs you cannot explain or defend.

With governance in place:

  • TCRO is built on consistent definitions and auditable data.
  • AI models consume data that has owners, quality checks, and access controls.

That changes what your systems can do:

  • TCRO becomes a living financial standard that can be shared with boards, lenders, and insurers.
  • Predictive operations move from pilot to portfolio scale because stakeholders trust the underlying data.

You can see how this plays out operationally in From Reactive to Predictive: Why Your Maintenance Model Is Now a Finance Strategy.

What does mature governance look like in practice?

Governance maturity tends to move through four stages:

  • Reactive. Siloed data, unclear owners, decisions made on assumptions. Result: constant rework and slow approvals.
  • Structured. Ownership defined, definitions agreed, basic quality checks. Result: fewer reconciliation fights, more trusted KPIs.
  • Governed. Lineage traceable, access controlled, audit trail built in. Result: defensible capital planning and stronger insurance submissions.
  • Predictive. Governed data feeding AI and risk models. Result: failures predicted before they occur, TCRO compressed year over year.

Most portfolios try to jump from stage 1 to stage 4 by buying technology. It does not work. AI scales whatever foundation you give it. If the foundation is confusion, AI just makes the confusion faster.

How does governance show up in financial results?

You know governance is working when you can see it in concrete outcomes:

  • The CFO, COO, and CRO walk into a board meeting using the same numbers.
  • Capital committee decks include traceable evidence, not screenshots from multiple systems.
  • Regulatory and audit questions are answered from existing records, not special projects.
  • Underwriters price based on continuous evidence of control rather than generic assumptions.
  • AI pilots produce consistent, explainable outputs because the input data is defined and permissioned.

This is what the TCRO dashboard should be built on: a clear, governed view of risk-related costs and trends. We describe that end-state in The TCRO Dashboard: What Your Board Needs to See, and What It Does Not.

How to put data governance on your board agenda

When this topic reaches the boardroom, resist the urge to start with technical frameworks. Start with pain.

Ask:

  • Where do we see different numbers for the same metric?
  • Where did we delay or water down a capital decision because we could not trust the data?
  • How much did we spend on emergency work we never budgeted?
  • How often did we scramble to assemble evidence for an audit, regulator, or insurer?

Then connect those points to the four governance elements: ownership, definitions, quality, and access. Translate governance from “data hygiene” into EBITDA protection and TCRO control.

For a board-ready way to frame this, see the CFO Risk-Adjusted Capital Planning Scorecard if you are using it alongside TCRO.

Where to go from here

If you want to move governance from idea to operating system, there are two practical moves:

  • Get a TCRO view that exposes the gaps. Use the TCRO calculator to model how downtime, emergency work, and insurance behave today. The places where you guess are the places where governance needs to improve.
  • Talk through your data reality with a specialist. Use the Talk to an Expert path in the contact modal to walk through how your current systems, reports, and owners line up – or do not – with the governance you need.

From there, data governance stops being an abstract IT initiative and becomes what it should be: the operating system of financial control for your portfolio.

title: From Reactive to Predictive: Why Your Maintenance Model Is Now a Finance Strategy
summary: Most building portfolios still run on a simple rule: something breaks, then people move. For decades, reactive maintenance was considered inevitable. It is not. It is a financial choice.
body:
Most building portfolios still run on a simple rule: something breaks, then people move. For decades, reactive maintenance was considered inevitable. It is not. It is a financial choice.

Reactive models create volatile operating expense, unpredictable downtime, and a weak risk story for insurers and boards. Time-based preventive programs help, but they still replace some assets too early and others too late. The real performance shift starts when you stop guessing and start using live data.

In commercial real estate, predictive maintenance is no longer an engineering experiment. It is a finance strategy.

What are the four maintenance models in real estate?

Across portfolios, maintenance strategies usually fall into one of four models:

  • Reactive. Wait for failure or tenant complaints.
  • Highest and least predictable cost.
  • Emergency dispatch, premium rates, reputational damage.
  • Preventive. Follow calendar or usage schedules.
  • Better than reactive, but still wasteful.
  • Good assets are replaced early, bad assets slip through.
  • Condition-based. Act when measured asset condition crosses thresholds.
  • More efficient.
  • Interventions are triggered by evidence, not dates.
  • Predictive. Use continuous data and analytics to forecast failure risk and expected impact.
  • Lowest total cost over time.
  • Interventions are timed to minimize Total Cost of Risk Ownership (TCRO) and disruption.

The model you choose determines how much unplanned risk, emergency spend, and avoidable outage you carry inside TCRO.

Why should CFOs care which model they run?

Maintenance models are often framed as technical choices. For a CFO or asset owner, they are TCRO choices.

Reactive environments drive:

  • Emergency repair premiums
  • Higher incident frequency and severity
  • Business interruption that never turns into a claim
  • Capital decisions made under duress instead of on a plan

Predictive environments reduce:

  • Unplanned failures that destroy budgets
  • Overtime and rush-order parts
  • Insurance friction at renewal
  • The gap between planned and actual capital timing

In other words, your maintenance model is a lever on operating margin, insurance outcomes, and asset value – not just a question for facilities.

For a deeper look at how this ties back into capital planning, see How TCRO Changes Capital Planning: From Age-Based Replacement to Risk-Weighted Investment.

How does condition-based maintenance bridge the gap?

You do not leap from reactive to predictive in one step. The practical bridge is condition-based maintenance.

Instead of relying purely on fixed schedules, you:

  • Instrument critical systems with sensors for vibration, temperature, pressure, and flow.
  • Define thresholds that reflect true risk, not nuisance alarms.
  • Trigger work orders when data crosses those thresholds.
  • Capture the intervention and the outcome in one shared system.

This approach:

  • Cuts obvious waste in preventive programs.
  • Produces the fault and intervention history you need to build reliable predictive models.
  • Builds trust in the data with operations and finance.

Condition-based maintenance is where governance, operations, and finance start to align. The same data that feeds maintenance decisions will later feed your TCRO metrics and TCRO dashboard.

What do predictive operations look like in the field?

Consider a 50-year-old events and entertainment campus that needed to modernize infrastructure without ripping it out.

The team focused on three areas:

  • Ice-making plant. Vibration monitoring on pumps and motors to detect early failure.
  • Water systems. Leak and flow detection on high-risk lines.
  • Gas and environment. Seismic shut-off and gas monitoring, noise and weather data for public safety.

The financial and risk outcomes were clear:

  • A single vibration alert on a key motor prevented a maximum probable loss of up to $5M in property damage, business interruption, and insurance impact.
  • A previously invisible natural gas leak worth more than $400K per year was discovered and stopped.
  • Lighting modernization tied into the same program cut electrical use by roughly 30% with a 4.5-year payback.
  • Annual insurance premium growth held at 0% in a hard market.

At a specialized dementia care facility, continuous commissioning and monitoring identified uncommissioned drives, misconfigured rooftop units, and under-performing heat exchangers. Fixing those issues produced six-figure handover savings and ongoing operating savings – while reducing outbreak risk and staffing strain.

These are not just engineering wins. They are TCRO shifts.

You can see the underlying stories in more depth in the case studies.

How do you move from reactive to predictive operations?

Moving to predictive operations is a sequence, not a switch.

A practical path:

  • Baseline and triage.
  • Build an inventory of critical assets.
  • Quantify emergency spend, downtime, and tenant impact over the last 12–24 months.
  • Prioritize systems by financial and human risk.
  • Standardize preventive work.
  • Clean up the basics.
  • Remove obvious waste in schedules and inconsistent practices.
  • Deploy condition monitoring.
  • Install monitoring on the highest-impact systems first.
  • Start with a small number of sites and assets where failures hurt most.
  • Integrate workflows.
  • Connect telemetry, work orders, and reporting into one data environment.
  • No more parallel spreadsheets or disconnected ticketing tools.
  • Build predictive models.
  • Use fault history and live data to forecast failure risk and expected impact.
  • Validate and refine models against real outcomes.
  • Scale across the portfolio.
  • Extend to more buildings and asset classes.
  • Tie insights back into capital planning, insurance strategy, and TCRO metrics.

This is not a technology project in isolation. It is an operating-model change that finance, operations, and risk should co-own.

For how governance underpins this, see Data Governance Is Not an IT Project. It Is the Operating System of Financial Control.

Where to go from here

If you want to understand what your current maintenance model is really costing you, there are two practical ways to begin:

  • Model the impact on TCRO. Use the TCRO calculator to plug in your current mix of failures, emergency work, and planned maintenance. Adjust the assumptions to reflect a more predictive model and see what that does to Total Cost of Risk Ownership.
  • Get a one-day, asset-level savings estimate. If you have a flagship building where reactive work is out of control, use Estimate Your Savings. Share one representative property and, within one business day, you will receive a reviewed estimate of what predictive operations could change for that asset’s risk and insurance profile.

Once you see the numbers in your own context, the choice of maintenance model stops being a technical debate and becomes what it really is: a financial decision about how much avoidable risk you are prepared to carry.

title: 7 NonNegotiable Criteria for a Digital Risk Platform That Actually Lowers TCRO
summary: There is no shortage of platforms promising “insights” and “dashboards.” Most do not move Total Cost of Risk Ownership (TCRO). They rearrange information you already have.
body:
There is no shortage of platforms promising “insights” and “dashboards.” Most do not move Total Cost of Risk Ownership (TCRO). They rearrange information you already have.

If you are a CFO, asset owner, or risk leader being pitched on digital risk, you need a different test: does this platform measurably lower TCRO and strengthen our story to boards, lenders, and insurers?

This is what to look for.

What is a digital risk platform?

A digital risk platform for institutional real estate connects building systems, sensors, and historical loss data into one environment. It identifies where failures are likely, quantifies what they will cost, and supports better insurance, maintenance, and capital decisions.

It sits above existing systems, not instead of them. It turns building and equipment risk data into a financial asset rather than a by-product.

For a deeper overview of how that fits into the broader risk and capital picture, see Total Cost of Risk Ownership vs Cost of Risk.

How do you choose a digital risk platform that actually lowers TCRO?

To know whether a platform will move TCRO, test it against seven non-negotiable criteria.

1. Claims-grounded risk models

Models cannot just be clever. They have to be right often enough that you are prepared to stake capital and insurance decisions on them.

Look for platforms that:

  • Are trained on years of claims and incident data across many buildings and equipment categories.
  • Connect live signals (vibration, temperature, air quality, flow) to expected losses with a probability and time window.
  • Can show you where their models have successfully predicted and prevented losses in the field.

If a platform’s models are built only on your own limited history or synthetic data, TCRO improvements will be difficult to trust.

2. Layered on existing infrastructure, not rip-and-replace

Most portfolios already have building management systems, meters, and point solutions. A viable digital risk platform must sit on top of that stack, not require you to rebuild it.

Ask each vendor:

  • Can you ingest data from our existing BMS, meters, and work-order systems via open APIs?
  • Where do we need additional sensors, and why?
  • How quickly do we see value without a multi-year rip-and-replace program?

If the answer sounds like a full rebuild, it is a capital project, not a digital risk platform.

3. A true common data environment

You need one source of truth for what you own, what is at risk, and what it costs if it fails.

That means the platform must:

  • Maintain a complete asset inventory – critical systems, age, condition, replacement cost, and business importance.
  • Normalize definitions for risk and performance across finance, operations, and risk.
  • Provide traceable lineage from raw data to the KPI a board sees on a dashboard.

Without this, you are still running multiple versions of reality. For a deeper dive into this foundation, see Data Governance Is Not an IT Project. It Is the Operating System of Financial Control.

4. TCRO baked into the design

Dashboards that stop at temperature curves and runtime hours are not enough. A digital risk platform should express risk in dollars – the language a CFO and board use.

Every alert and scenario should answer three questions:

  • What asset is at risk?
  • What is the probability of failure in a defined window?
  • What is the expected financial impact if we do nothing?

If the interface cannot connect a signal to the components of TCRO – losses, downtime, premiums, operating inefficiency, compliance – it will be hard to justify in a budget cycle. For how TCRO works as a framework, see Total Cost of Risk Ownership vs Cost of Risk.

5. Insurance-grade evidence and partner alignment

Digital risk does not exist in isolation. It sits in a triangle between owners, brokers, and carriers.

A credible platform should:

  • Produce audit-ready histories of risk conditions, interventions, and outcomes.
  • Export the views underwriters care about: evidence of reduced frequency and severity, not just system uptime.
  • Fit into program design so that better performance can earn better terms at renewal.

That is the difference between “nice reports” and “insurance behaves like a controllable lever inside TCRO.” For more on this angle, see Insurance Is No Longer a Fixed Line Item.

6. Automated workflows with a closed loop

It is not enough to identify risk. You have to prove you acted.

The platform should:

  • Trigger clear, prioritized workflows when conditions change.
  • Integrate with your existing work-order and ticketing tools so teams are not managing two systems.
  • Time-stamp actions and link them back to the original risk signal.
  • Track outcomes and feed them back into models.

Without this, you have alerts, not risk management.

7. Human-level outcomes, not just technical uptime

Finally, do not lose sight of why any of this matters.

Behind every avoided failure is:

  • A resident who did not endure a lockdown or outage.
  • A staff team that did not work a crisis shift.
  • A visitor who did not have their event cancelled at the last minute.

The best digital risk platforms make it easy to tell that story:

  • Case studies that name the avoided loss, the TCRO impact, and the people affected.
  • Dashboards that can show both financial outcomes and basic well-being indicators (outbreak weeks, incident counts, complaint trends).

You can see examples of this kind of story in the case studies.

How to use these criteria in your buying process

Treat these seven criteria as your short list in any digital risk platform decision.

In a vendor conversation:

  • Ask each provider to score themselves against all seven and provide proof.
  • Ask for customer references where the platform has reduced TCRO or insurance outcomes in measurable ways.
  • Map their answers against your own portfolio strategy and TCRO priorities.

From there, you can sort vendors into three buckets:

  • Platforms that primarily visualize what you already know.
  • Platforms that can reduce some operational noise but do not tie to TCRO.
  • Platforms that measurably change risk-related costs and support better capital and insurance decisions.

You are looking for the third bucket.

Where to go from here

If you are actively evaluating options, there are two practical next steps:

  • See the criteria in action. Use Book a Demo to see how these seven elements show up in the Novem Digital platform – from claims-grounded models to insurance-grade evidence and TCRO reporting.
  • Run your own numbers first. If you prefer to quantify the opportunity before a demo, use Estimate Your Savings to get a one-day, human-reviewed estimate for a flagship asset, or use the TCRO calculator to model portfolio-level impact.

Once you have both a clear set of criteria and your own numbers, it becomes much easier to tell which digital risk platform is just another dashboard – and which one will actually lower TCRO and protect both your returns and the people inside your buildings.
